title: Data Journalism Fellow/Associate Fellow
gist: A Lawfare hiring post that doubles as a product disclosure: its RAGtime AI research platform, live in production since May 2026 with 1.6M+ primary-source legal/government documents, is moving from a supporter beta toward public release, signaling AI-native disruption of legal and public-records research.

insights.claim: Lawfare's RAGtime AI research platform went into production in May 2026 and integrates more than 1.6 million primary-source documents (federal court litigation, U.S. Code, CFR, OLC opinions, and U.S. foreign-relations history) into a single searchable, queryable interface.
insights.speaker: Tyler McBrien
insights.so_what: A nonprofit newsroom fielding a 1.6M-document RAG platform signals that AI-native primary-source research is becoming cheap enough to disrupt incumbents like Westlaw and LexisNexis.
insights.locator: We are pleased to announce that Lawfare is hiring

insights.claim: RAGtime launched a beta in June 2026 to Lawfare's roughly 10,000 financial supporters and is now moving toward wider public release.
insights.speaker: Tyler McBrien
insights.so_what: The supporter-beta-to-public-release path suggests a potential product or spin-out; the 10,000-supporter base is the initial distribution.
insights.locator: A beta version launched in June 2026

insights.claim: Lawfare explicitly designs the data-journalism roles around 'vibe coding'—using AI tools to write lightweight code—stating formal software-engineering experience is not required to build and maintain data pipelines.
insights.speaker: Tyler McBrien
insights.so_what: AI-assisted coding is collapsing the technical barrier for non-engineers to build data products, a labor-market and tooling signal.
insights.locator: Help keep current and new data pipelines up to date
